  mu-lus-gor-mu-lus-ti -> ro-gor-fen-ro-lus-mu
  ro-ro-fen-mu-fen-lus -> ke-ke-ti-ro-lus-gor
  ti-mu-lus-ti-ti-ti -> mu-ro-gor-mu-lus-mu

Give one input that the machine prints as ke-fen-gor-ro-lus-lus.
Any input of exactly 6 glyphs that works is accepted.
Write glyph strings dash-joined, e.g. za-ke-ro.

ANSWER: <value>

Probe 2: mu-lus-gor-mu-lus-ti -> mu-lus-gor-mu-za-ti -> ro-gor-fen-ro-lus-mu -- matches the log.

So the rule: first overwrite slot 5 with za, then step every glyph 7 forward on the cycle.
Now work backward from the target ke-fen-gor-ro-lus-lus.
Undo the +7 shift: stepping every glyph of ke-fen-gor-ro-lus-lus back 7 gives ro-gor-lus-mu-za-za.
The overwrite forces slot 5 to za, and ro-gor-lus-mu-za-za indeed shows za there. Before the overwrite that slot could have held anything; I take fen and keep the rest: ro-gor-lus-mu-fen-za.

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

Exactly ONE rule of this patchwheel was corrupted:

1. every zim becomes sabb
2. drop after dree
3. drop after zim
4. drop after tosk

Evidence (input => ORIGINAL wheel output):
E1: zim tosk sabb tosk zim => sabb tosk | shown wheel gives sabb tosk tosk
E2: fole sabb tosk dree zim => fole sabb tosk | shown wheel: match

Which rule number was corrupted? (If replacing a different single
rule could also make every evidence tape match, that number is
accepted too.)

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
Let me find the corrupted rule in this 4-rule wheel.
The evidence says E2 matches under the shown wheel, while E1 comes out wrong.
Whatever repair I settle on has to keep E2 matching too.

First, E1 under the shown wheel, one rule at a time.
Start: zim tosk sabb tosk zim.
Rule 1 is every zim becomes sabb, giving sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 2 is drop after dree, giving sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 3 is drop after zim, giving sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 4 is drop after tosk, giving sabb tosk tosk.
That leaves sabb tosk tosk, but the original wheel gave sabb tosk.
My tape has 3 tokens; the original's has 2.
So the corruption changes how many tokens survive.

Since just one rule was tampered with, one well-chosen repair has to fix every example at once.
Rule 1 is a dead end: the tape reaching it in E1 is zim tosk sabb tosk zim, and no single replacement there makes all 2 examples match.
Rule 4 fails the same way.
Consider rule 3, currently 'drop after zim'. Test it as 'drop after tosk'.
Rules 1 and 2 are untouched, so the tape entering slot 3 is still sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 3 is drop after tosk, giving sabb tosk tosk.
Rule 4 is drop after tosk, giving sabb tosk.
Final: sabb tosk - exactly what the original wheel gave for E1.

Does that repair hold everywhere?
E2: fole sabb tosk dree zim comes out fole sabb tosk; the original gave fole sabb tosk - match.

With rule 3 restored, everything matches. The corrupted rule is number 3.

SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)

messages.content:
A sealed glyph machine transforms any string of exactly 4 glyphs
by one fixed hidden rule.
The glyph alphabet, in cycle order: za, ke, ro, mu, ti, fen, gor, lus (after lus the cycle wraps back to za).
The hidden rule is ONE of: (a) the whole string is reversed; (b) the glyphs at two secret fixed positions trade places; (c) one secret fixed position is overwritten with one secret fixed glyph; (d) every glyph steps forward the same secret count along the alphabet cycle.

Probe log (input -> output):
  lus-mu-fen-ti -> lus-fen-mu-ti
  ro-mu-mu-lus -> ro-mu-mu-lus
  lus-ti-fen-ke -> lus-fen-ti-ke

Give one input that the machine prints as ti-gor-lus-ro.
Any input of exactly 4 glyphs that works is accepted.
Write glyph strings dash-joined, e.g. za-ke-ro.

ANSWER: <value>

think:
I need an input that the machine prints as ti-gor-lus-ro.
I'll walk the rule menu in order: hypothesize a family, verify it on every probe pair, and eliminate on the first mismatch.
Cycle order: za, ke, ro, mu, ti, fen, gor, lus, then back around to za.
Reversal? Reversed, probe 1's input would read ti-fen-mu-lus, but the log shows lus-fen-mu-ti -- slot 1 is lus, not ti. Not reversal.
A swap: probe 1 changes exactly slots 2 and 3, trading mu and fen.
Probe 2 holds mu in both slots, so it rightly comes back unchanged.
Probe 3: swapping those slots gives lus-fen-ti-ke -- matches.
An overwrite? Probe 1 changes slots 2 and 3 at once, and an overwrite touches exactly one slot. No.
A cycle-shift? On probe 1, lus to lus is +0, but slot 2 goes mu to fen, which is +2. A shift moves every glyph by the same count, so no.
So the rule: swap slots 2 and 3.
Now work backward from the target ti-gor-lus-ro.

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

1. every quom becomes zim
2. swap first last
3. swap first last

E3: zim tosk yeg quom tosk zim => zim tosk yeg tosk tosk zim | shown wheel gives zim tosk yeg zim tosk zim

by one fixed hidden rule.
The glyph alphabet, in cycle order: za, ke, ro, mu, ti, fen, gor, lus (after lus the cycle wraps back to za).
The hidden rule is ONE of: (a) the whole string is reversed; (b) the glyphs at two secret fixed positions trade places; (c) one secret fixed position is overwritten with one secret fixed glyph; (d) every glyph steps forward the same secret count along the alphabet cycle.

Probe log (input -> output):
  ti-lus-fen-lus -> lus-fen-lus-ti
  fen-fen-ro-fen -> fen-ro-fen-fen
  ke-za-ti-lus -> lus-ti-za-ke

Give one input that the machine prints as ti-ti-ro-za.

I'll walk the rule menu in order: hypothesize a family, verify it on every probe pair, and eliminate on the first mismatch.
Cycle order: za, ke, ro, mu, ti, fen, gor, lus, then back around to za.
Reversal: probe 1 reversed reads lus-fen-lus-ti -- exactly what the machine printed.
Probe 2 reversed gives fen-ro-fen-fen -- matches.
Probe 3 reversed gives lus-ti-za-ke -- matches.
A swap? Probe 1 changes 4 slots, and a single swap changes exactly two slots or none. No.
An overwrite? Probe 1 changes slots 1 and 2 at once, and an overwrite touches exactly one slot. No.
A cycle-shift? On probe 1, ti to lus is +3, but slot 2 goes lus to fen, which is +6. A shift moves every glyph by the same count, so no.
That settles the rule: reverse the whole string.
Now work backward from the target ti-ti-ro-za.
Undo the reversal: ti-ti-ro-za un-reversed is za-ro-ti-ti.
Check forward: za-ro-ti-ti -> ti-ti-ro-za -- that lands exactly on the target. Good.
So the answer is za-ro-ti-ti.

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

Exactly ONE rule of this patchwheel was corrupted:

1. drop after plim
2. swap first last
3. drop before ruv

Evidence (input => ORIGINAL wheel output):
E1: ruv plim nack nack => nack plim ruv | shown wheel gives nack ruv
E2: yeg ruv ruv yeg => yeg ruv yeg | shown wheel gives ruv yeg

Which rule number was corrupted? (If replacing a different single
rule could also make every evidence tape match, that number is
accepted too.)

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

Only one rule is corrupted, so I look for a slot where one replacement squares all the evidence.
Rule 1 is a dead end: the tape reaching it in E1 is ruv plim nack nack, and no single replacement there makes all 2 examples match.
Rule 2 fails the same way.
My suspicion is rule 3, shown as 'drop before ruv'. What if the original was 'drop after ruv'?
Rules 1 and 2 are untouched, so the tape entering slot 3 is still nack plim ruv.
Apply rule 3 (drop after ruv): nack plim ruv.
Final: nack plim ruv - exactly what the original wheel gave for E1.

Cross-check the rest with rule 3 repaired.
E2: yeg ruv ruv yeg comes out yeg ruv yeg; the original gave yeg ruv yeg - match.

That single repair squares all 2 examples. So the corrupted rule is rule 3.

messages.content:
SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

Below is the BUGGY copy of a loom-house routine (exactly one line differs from the correct copy). Trace it exactly as printed.

1: IFZ A
2: ADD A 1
3: SWAP D C
4: SUB C 4
5: COPY D B
6: ADD A 5

Start registers: A=0 B=7 C=9 D=1.
After the routine finishes, what value does register B hold?

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
Let me execute this routine carefully, one line at a time, keeping an eye on register B.
I begin with A=0 B=7 C=9 D=1.
The label says this is the buggy copy, but the question asks what this printed routine leaves in B, so I trace it exactly as shown.

Scanning first: no line ever touches B directly, so it should keep its start value — I'll trace the whole routine anyway to be sure.

Line 1 is IFZ A: A is 0, so line 2 (ADD A 1) is skipped. Now A=0 B=7 C=9 D=1.
Line 3 is SWAP D C: now D=9 and C=1. Now A=0 B=7 C=1 D=9.
Line 4 is SUB C 4: 1 minus 4 would go negative, so C floors at 0. Now A=0 B=7 C=0 D=9.
Line 5 is COPY D B: D takes B's value, so D=7. Now A=0 B=7 C=0 D=7.
Line 6 is ADD A 5: A goes from 0 to 5. Now A=5 B=7 C=0 D=7.
That was the last line, so the routine ends with A=5 B=7 C=0 D=7.

Before I commit, let me double-check B's history.
No line ever changed B, so it stays at 7 from start to finish. That matches my trace.
Register B's final value is 7.

messages.content:
SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

Below is the BUGGY copy of a loom-house routine (exactly one line differs from the correct copy). Trace it exactly as printed.

1: ADD B 2
2: ZERO B
3: SUB D 3
4: ZERO C
5: ZERO A
6: SWAP C B
7: COPY C A
8: SWAP D B

Start registers: A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
After the routine finishes, what value does register B hold?

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
I need to run this routine exactly as printed and report what register B holds when it finishes.
I begin with A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
The label says this is the buggy copy, but the question asks what this printed routine leaves in B, so I trace it exactly as shown.

Scanning first: lines 1, 2, 6, 8 touch B; I'll still trace the whole routine in order to keep every register straight.

Line 1 is ADD B 2: B goes from 0 to 2. Now A=4 B=2 C=5 D=0.
Line 2 is ZERO B: B clears from 2 to 0. Now A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
Line 3 is SUB D 3: 0 minus 3 would go negative, so D floors at 0. Now A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
Line 4 is ZERO C: C clears from 5 to 0. Now A=4 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 5 is ZERO A: A clears from 4 to 0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 6 is SWAP C B: now C=0 and B=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 7 is COPY C A: C takes A's value, so C=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 8 is SWAP D B: now D=0 and B=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
That was the last line, so the routine ends with A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.

Quick second pass on B to be safe.
B begins at 0, then line 1 (ADD B 2) makes it 2, then line 2 (ZERO B) makes it 0, and it never changes again. That matches my trace.
So at the end of the routine, B holds 0.
